examples.title: Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of Dijkstra's algorithm for finding shortest paths in a network graph
examples.code:
DIJKSTRA(Graph, Source):
 FOR each vertex v in Graph:
 distance[v] = INFINITY
 previous[v] = UNDEFINED
 visited[v] = FALSE
 distance[Source] = 0
 
 WHILE all vertices are not visited:
 // Select unvisited vertex with minimum distance
 u = vertex with minimum distance[u] among unvisited vertices
 visited[u] = TRUE
 
 // Update distances of neighbors
 FOR each neighbor v of u:
 IF NOT visited[v]:
 alt = distance[u] + weight(u, v)
 IF alt < distance[v]:
 distance[v] = alt
 previous[v] = u
 
 RETURN distance, previous
examples.explanation: This algorithm finds the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted graph. It uses a greedy approach, always selecting the node with minimum distance. Used by OSPF and IS-IS protocols.
examples.timeComplexity: O(E log V) with min-heap, O(V²) without

examples.title: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
examples.description: Shortest path algorithm that can handle negative edge weights, used in Distance Vector protocols
examples.code:
BELLMAN_FORD(Graph, Source):
 FOR each vertex v in Graph:
 distance[v] = INFINITY
 previous[v] = UNDEFINED
 distance[Source] = 0
 
 // Relax edges V-1 times
 FOR i = 1 to |V| - 1:
 FOR each edge (u, v) in Graph:
 IF distance[u] + weight(u, v) < distance[v]:
 distance[v] = distance[u] + weight(u, v)
 previous[v] = u
 
 // Check for negative cycles
 FOR each edge (u, v) in Graph:
 IF distance[u] + weight(u, v) < distance[v]:
 RETURN error: "Negative cycle detected"
 
 RETURN distance, previous
examples.explanation: Bellman-Ford algorithm is used in distance-vector routing protocols. It can handle negative weights unlike Dijkstra. The algorithm performs |V|-1 iterations to find shortest paths.

examples.title: Distance Vector Routing Update Process
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how routers update their routing tables using Distance Vector protocol
examples.code:
// Router receives update from neighbor
PROCESS_UPDATE(neighbor, neighbor_table):
 FOR each destination in neighbor_table:
 new_metric = neighbor_table[destination].metric + 1
 
 // Check if better route found
 IF new_metric < routing_table[destination].metric:
 routing_table[destination].metric = new_metric
 routing_table[destination].next_hop = neighbor
 routing_table[destination].interface = incoming_interface
 TRIGGER_UPDATE()
 
 // Check if next hop is same, update even if worse
 ELSE IF neighbor == routing_table[destination].next_hop:
 routing_table[destination].metric = new_metric
 TRIGGER_UPDATE()

// Periodic Update Timer
PERIODIC_TIMER():
 FOR each neighbor:
 SEND_UPDATE(neighbor, routing_table)
examples.explanation: This demonstrates how Distance Vector protocols process routing updates. Each router adds 1 to the metric (hop count) received from neighbors and updates its table if a shorter path is found.
